Phase-40.NAME: Official MTProxy data-plane and individual user secrets
Phase-40.intent: Replace the unreliable KPprotoN/Erlang MTProto data-plane with the official Telegram MTProxy runtime while keeping KrotPN as the user, assignment, secret, admin, and deployment control-plane.

scope.in-scope-1: Official MTProxy runtime on the DE data-plane node.
scope.in-scope-2: One individual official MTProxy secret per verified KrotPN user.
scope.in-scope-3: Secret-safe KrotPN sync from active assignments into the official runtime.
scope.in-scope-4: RU public TCP 443 routing that keeps HTTPS on krotpn.xyz working and sends MTProto traffic to the DE runtime.
scope.in-scope-5: Owner-only user cabinet payloads and admin-safe redacted operations.
scope.in-scope-6: Live Telegram req_pq verification through the public RU edge.
scope.out-of-scope-1: No redesign of the whole installer/deploy workflow beyond Phase-40 MTProxy wiring.
scope.out-of-scope-2: No exposure of raw proxy links, private keys, runtime tokens, base secrets, or user secrets in logs or admin views.

driver-findings.finding-1: Phase-39 proved DNS, RU public 443 reachability, HAProxy routing, DE policy health, fake-TLS acceptance, and Telegram downstream reachability, but real Telegram clients still marked issued links unavailable.
driver-findings.finding-2: A stronger client-level req_pq probe timed out against the KPprotoN/Erlang runtime, so fake-TLS acceptance alone is not a valid production availability proof.
driver-findings.finding-3: A temporary official MTProxy runtime on the same DE host passed req_pq for normal and secure MTProxy secrets, proving the blocker is the current KrotPN data-plane implementation rather than the server network path.

modules.module-1: M-052 official-mtproxy-data-plane owns the official runtime, redacted launcher, stats/health, and RU-to-DE data-plane routing contract.
modules.module-2: M-053 mtproxy-secret-sync owns per-user official secret derivation, manifest rendering, apply/revoke/reissue, and replay after restart or restore.
modules.module-3: M-042 preserves restore-safe assignment metadata and adds credential mode/version fields as needed.
modules.module-4: M-043 changes provisioning output from fake-TLS SNI-derived links to official secure MTProxy links.
modules.module-5: M-045 updates the user cabinet to present one compact official Telegram proxy action.
modules.module-6: M-047 keeps admin MTProto operations redacted while targeting official secret lifecycle actions.
modules.module-7: M-050 updates the RU edge from issued fake-TLS SNI routing to HTTPS-vs-MTProto TCP classification.
modules.module-8: M-051 promotes req_pq-based smoke to the production gate.
modules.module-9: M-012 wires the official runtime into the existing deploy surface without broad installer redesign.

architecture.flow-1: Verified email activation creates or reuses one canonical MTProto assignment for the user.
architecture.flow-2: KrotPN derives one 16-byte official MTProxy user secret and exposes only the owner payload as secret=dd<hex-secret>.
architecture.flow-3: M-053 renders active official secrets into a redacted runtime manifest and applies it to M-052.
architecture.flow-4: M-052 launches official mtproto-proxy with one -S argument per active user secret and keeps stats local/private.
architecture.flow-5: RU public 443 sends HTTPS ClientHello for krotpn.xyz and admin-safe web names to nginx, while non-HTTPS MTProto traffic is sent to the DE official MTProxy runtime.
architecture.flow-6: User links may keep stable user wildcard hostnames, but identity and access are controlled by the official per-user secret, not fake-TLS SNI policy.

steps.step-1: Create Phase-40 MyGRACE module and verification artifacts for M-052 and M-053.
steps.step-2: Add official credential mode and deterministic, per-user secret derivation without logging or persisting unnecessary raw secret material.
steps.step-3: Implement secret sync apply/revoke/reissue/replay semantics with degraded-safe user and admin status.
steps.step-4: Add official MTProxy runtime compose/launcher that bypasses secret-printing wrapper behavior and accepts the full active secret manifest.
steps.step-5: Update RU 443 routing so normal HTTPS remains stable and MTProto traffic reaches DE official MTProxy.
steps.step-6: Update user and admin MTProto API/UX wording and actions for official secure MTProxy links.
steps.step-7: Add unit/static/smoke tests for secret derivation, manifest redaction, routing contract, and runtime launcher behavior.
steps.step-8: Add live req_pq smoke through the public RU edge without printing full links or secrets.
steps.step-9: Run clean local verification, MyGRACE refresh/reviewer, then prepare a test release only after live Telegram Desktop/phone proof passes.

acceptance.item-1: New verified user receives an individual official MTProxy secret and secure Telegram link.
acceptance.item-2: Telegram Desktop and mobile mark the issued proxy available through the public KrotPN domain on port 443.
acceptance.item-3: Automated live req_pq smoke passes through RU public 443 to the DE official runtime.
acceptance.item-4: Web HTTPS, admin 8443, backend API, and existing VPN functions remain available.
acceptance.item-5: Admin revoke removes exactly one user secret from runtime access; reissue rotates exactly one user secret.
acceptance.item-6: No raw user secret, full proxy link, runtime token, private key, or provider secret appears in logs, smoke output, admin tables, or audit records.

risk-controls.control-1: Legacy fake-TLS links must be treated as superseded and reissued explicitly; do not silently claim compatibility.
risk-controls.control-2: Official Docker wrapper output is not trusted for production because it prints links/secrets; use a redacted launcher around the official binary.
risk-controls.control-3: Restart-on-secret-change is acceptable for the first production closure, but the implementation must be idempotent and visible as degraded during apply failure.
risk-controls.control-4: Secret manifest size and command-line argument limits must be guarded before scale-out; shard/runtime split is a later phase if needed.
risk-controls.control-5: Shared public 443 changes require rollback evidence before release tagging.

verification-refs.verification-1: V-M-052 validates official runtime, redacted launcher, routing, stats, and live req_pq behavior.
verification-refs.verification-2: V-M-053 validates secret derivation, owner-only payloads, manifest sync, revoke, reissue, replay, and redaction.

verification-design.matrix-1: Deterministic user secret derivation, credential mode migration, owner-only secure link, unverified-user guard, manifest redaction, idempotent apply, revoke, reissue, replay, and degraded retry behavior.
verification-design.matrix-2: Official MTProxy launcher syntax, official -S argument rendering, no wrapper secret printing, private stats binding, runtime config fetch/cache fallback, and health output redaction.
verification-design.matrix-3: Deploy/compose/HAProxy static checks prove public 443 ownership, HTTPS ClientHello routing to web, non-HTTPS MTProto routing to DE, admin 8443 preservation, and rollback path.
verification-design.matrix-4: API and UI checks prove official secure payload shape, legacy fake-TLS reissue messaging, admin redaction, and single-user revoke/reissue blast radius.
verification-design.matrix-5: Redacted live smoke obtains one active issued assignment, confirms runtime secret sync, sends req_pq through RU public 443 to DE official MTProxy, and records Telegram Desktop/mobile available proof.

autonomous-execution-safety.safety-1: Safe for local autonomous execution after verification artifacts are in place: backend unit tests, static deploy tests, frontend build, XML parse, and MyGRACE lint.
autonomous-execution-safety.safety-2: Not safe for unattended live promotion: public 443 routing, DE runtime replacement, and Telegram client proof require explicit operator confirmation and rollback readiness.
autonomous-execution-safety.safety-3: Any evidence that prints raw secrets, full tg links, wildcard private keys, runtime tokens, or provider keys invalidates the run and requires cleanup before retry.
